claims.claim_text: The CrowdStrike outage began in Austin, Texas at 04:09 UTC on July 19, 2024.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters
claims.rationale: The exact location and timestamp are load-bearing incident specifics that are not supported by an allowlisted source here.

claims.claim_text: A routine CrowdStrike Falcon update escalated into one of the largest IT outages in global history.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters | first paragraph
claims.anchor_text: What began as a routine sensor configuration update pushed by CrowdStrike to its Falcon sensor quickly escalated into one of the largest IT outages in global history.
claims.suggested_rewrite: What began as a routine CrowdStrike Falcon content update quickly escalated into a major global IT outage.
claims.rationale: The 'one of the largest in global history' ranking is not directly backed by a primary source in the allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Microsoft estimated that the CrowdStrike outage affected about 8.5 million Windows devices worldwide.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters | first paragraph
claims.rationale: This is a real, citable incident figure, but the primary source is on an off-allowlist Microsoft blog host.

claims.claim_text: Direct losses from the CrowdStrike outage were estimated at more than $5.4 billion.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters | first paragraph
claims.anchor_text: The financial impact was staggering, with direct losses estimated at over $5.4 billion.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The financial impact was severe, with affected organizations reporting major losses and recovery costs.
claims.rationale: This exact estimate is a floating third-party figure and should not remain without a precise primary source.

claims.claim_text: The outage grounded thousands of flights, canceled critical surgeries, and halted global banking infrastructure.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters | first paragraph
claims.anchor_text: Airlines grounded thousands of flights, hospitals canceled critical surgeries, and global banking infrastructure ground to a halt.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Airlines, hospitals, banks, and other critical sectors experienced widespread disruption.
claims.rationale: This is a composite real-world impact claim with multiple precise specifics and no single primary source in the allowlist.

claims.claim_text: CrowdStrike's RCA attributed the crash to an out-of-bounds memory read triggered by Channel File 291 content.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters | second paragraph
claims.rationale: The technical RCA is real and primary, but CrowdStrike's PDF host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: An AI causal graph could have instantaneously correlated the Channel File 291 deployment with host failures and halted rollout before global saturation.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters | third paragraph
claims.anchor_text: An AI-driven causal graph could have instantaneously correlated the sub-millisecond deployment of the `C-00000291*.sys` channel file with the cascading host offline metrics, halting the staggered rollout before it reached global saturation.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Automated rollout controls could have correlated the deployment with a sudden wave of host failures and stopped further rollout before the blast radius grew.
claims.rationale: This counterfactual uses unsupported precision and the filename detail is not presented accurately enough to keep as written.

claims.claim_text: A moderately sized startup with microservices generates roughly 50 to 100 GB of logs per day.
claims.span_hint: section: The Fundamental Problem
claims.anchor_text: A moderately sized startup with a microservice architecture generates roughly 50 to 100 GB of logs per day.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Even a modest microservice deployment can generate a large daily volume of logs.
claims.rationale: This teaching statistic is precise but uncited and should be rewritten without unsupported volume numbers.

claims.claim_text: Enterprise platforms and hyperscalers routinely ingest over 1 PB of log data daily.
claims.span_hint: section: The Fundamental Problem
claims.anchor_text: Enterprise platforms and hyperscalers routinely ingest over 1 PB (petabyte) of log data daily.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Large platforms can ingest log volumes at petabyte scale.
claims.rationale: This is an uncited quantitative scale claim and should lose the precise daily figure.

claims.claim_text: At one petabyte per day, the system handles approximately 10 billion individual log events.
claims.span_hint: section: The Fundamental Problem
claims.anchor_text: At one petabyte per day, you are looking at approximately 10 billion individual log events.
claims.suggested_rewrite: At petabyte-scale ingestion, the number of individual log events can reach into the billions.
claims.rationale: This conversion depends on unstated assumptions and should not remain as a precise fact.

claims.claim_text: Datadog released Toto on May 21, 2025 as a 151 million parameter open-weights time-series foundation model under Apache 2.0.
claims.span_hint: section: LLM-Powered Parsing | Did You Know
claims.rationale: This is a real vendor release with specific date, size, and license details, but the primary source is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Prometheus became the second CNCF project to graduate in August 2018, after Kubernetes.
claims.span_hint: section: LLM-Based Detection | Did You Know
claims.rationale: The CNCF announcement directly states that Prometheus was the second project to graduate, following Kubernetes.

claims.claim_text: An AIOps engine can isolate temporal correlations across thousands of metric streams in milliseconds.
claims.span_hint: section: The RCA Challenge vs. AI RCA
claims.anchor_text: The AIOps engine ingests thousands of metric streams simultaneously, isolating temporal correlations in milliseconds.
claims.suggested_rewrite: An AIOps engine can analyze many metric streams in parallel and surface likely correlations much faster than a manual investigation.
claims.rationale: The milliseconds performance claim is illustrative but uncited and should not remain as a precise benchmark.

claims.claim_text: Gartner renamed its AIOps market guide to 'Market Guide for Event Intelligence Solutions' on March 10, 2025 and framed the space around augmentation, acceleration, and automation.
claims.span_hint: section: Automation Levels and Safety Guardrails | Did You Know
claims.rationale: This named analyst reference is real and citable, but Gartner is off the allowlist.

claims.claim_text: New Relic was taken private in November 2023 in a transaction valued at $6.5 billion.
claims.span_hint: section: Commercial Platforms and Open Source | Did You Know
claims.rationale: This corporate event is real and directly documented, but the primary release host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: New Relic reported in 2026 that its AIOps users shipped code at an 80% higher frequency.
claims.span_hint: section: Commercial Platforms and Open Source | Did You Know
claims.rationale: The exact percentage comes from a primary vendor report, but the domain is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: AWS DevOps Guru uses machine learning to analyze operational data and surface operational issues and risks.
claims.span_hint: section: Commercial Platforms and Open Source | AIOPS PLATFORMS (2024) block
claims.rationale: The AWS user guide directly describes DevOps Guru as a machine-learning-based operational insights service.

claims.claim_text: Azure Monitor provides smart detection alerts for anomalous application behavior.
claims.span_hint: section: Commercial Platforms and Open Source | AIOPS PLATFORMS (2024) block
claims.rationale: Microsoft Learn documents Azure Monitor smart detection alerts and anomaly-oriented diagnostics behavior.

claims.claim_text: Google Cloud Operations provides integrated monitoring and logging services.
claims.span_hint: section: Commercial Platforms and Open Source | AIOPS PLATFORMS (2024) block
claims.rationale: Google Cloud's product page directly describes the offering as integrated monitoring, logging, and trace services.

claims.claim_text: As of April 2026, OpenTelemetry traces, metrics, and logs were fully stable and profiles remained in development.
claims.span_hint: section: The State of OpenTelemetry (2026)
claims.anchor_text: As of April 2026, OpenTelemetry traces, metrics, and logs have all reached full stability and are widely adopted in production. Conversely, the continuous profiling signal (*profiles*) remains in active development status.
claims.suggested_rewrite: As of April 2026, the OpenTelemetry specification status summary marks traces, metrics, and logging as stable, while profiles remain in development.
claims.rationale: The official status page supports the maturity states but not the stronger 'widely adopted in production' wording.

claims.claim_text: Teams should require human approval for at least 30 days before automating remediation.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes in AIOps Adoption | table row Automating Level 4 immediately
claims.anchor_text: Require human approval for at least 30 days before automating.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Require a sustained period of successful human-approved remediations before automating.
claims.rationale: The exact 30-day threshold is prescriptive false precision without a cited standard.

claims.claim_text: Vendors claim 90%+ alert-noise reduction.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes in AIOps Adoption | table row Trusting vendor correlations
claims.anchor_text: Vendors claim 90%+ noise reduction, confusing correlation with causation.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Vendors often market substantial noise reduction, so validate the claimed benefit against your own incident history and baselines.
claims.rationale: The 90%+ figure is a floating marketing statistic and should not remain as an uncited fact.

claims.claim_text: Splunk ITSI provides machine-learning-driven IT service intelligence and incident analysis capabilities.
claims.span_hint: section: Commercial Platforms and Open Source | AIOPS PLATFORMS (2024) block
claims.rationale: This vendor capability claim is real and documented on Splunk's official product page, which is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Datadog Watchdog provides AI-based anomaly detection and root-cause assistance.
claims.span_hint: section: Commercial Platforms and Open Source | AIOPS PLATFORMS (2024) block
claims.rationale: The Datadog docs directly back the capability, but the host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Dynatrace Davis provides AI-powered root cause analysis over causal topology data.
claims.span_hint: section: Commercial Platforms and Open Source | AIOPS PLATFORMS (2024) block
claims.rationale: Dynatrace's official docs support this product capability, but the domain is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: New Relic offers Applied Intelligence and AIOps capabilities for anomaly detection, correlation, and root-cause analysis.
claims.span_hint: section: Commercial Platforms and Open Source | AIOPS PLATFORMS (2024) block
claims.rationale: This is an official vendor capability page on an off-allowlist host.

claims.claim_text: ServiceNow ITOM integrates AIOps capabilities with ITSM-oriented workflows.
claims.span_hint: section: Commercial Platforms and Open Source | AIOPS PLATFORMS (2024) block
claims.rationale: The official ServiceNow product page backs the integration claim, but the domain is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Moogsoft is a pioneer in AI incident management.
claims.span_hint: section: Commercial Platforms and Open Source | AIOPS PLATFORMS (2024) block
claims.rationale: The 'pioneer' label is a superlative/judgment claim without a benchmark or authoritative source.

claims.claim_text: BigPanda provides event correlation and automation for incident operations.
claims.span_hint: section: Commercial Platforms and Open Source | AIOPS PLATFORMS (2024) block
claims.rationale: The vendor docs support this capability, but the host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: PagerDuty provides AIOps capabilities for intelligent incident response, triage, and noise reduction.
claims.span_hint: section: Commercial Platforms and Open Source | AIOPS PLATFORMS (2024) block
claims.rationale: PagerDuty's official documentation backs the capability, but the domain is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: OpsRamp provides AIOps capabilities for hybrid infrastructure operations.
claims.span_hint: section: Commercial Platforms and Open Source | AIOPS PLATFORMS (2024) block
claims.rationale: This capability is documented on the vendor's official site, which is off-allowlist.

further_reading.title: OpenTelemetry Specification Status Summary
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful for verifying which telemetry signals are stable enough to treat as production-grade inputs to an AIOps stack.

further_reading.title: CNCF Announces Prometheus Graduation
further_reading.why_relevant: Provides historical context on Prometheus as a core metrics foundation in modern observability pipelines.

further_reading.title: What Is Amazon DevOps Guru?
further_reading.why_relevant: Gives a concrete example of a managed AIOps-style service and the kinds of ML-backed operational insights vendors actually document.

notes: Exercise tasks, quiz prompts, Mermaid diagrams, and code samples were excluded. I included single-line vendor-matrix entries only where the line itself asserted a concrete product capability or a superlative that would need sourcing or cleanup.
